steps.title: Condition for infinitely many solutions
steps.content: A system has infinitely many solutions when both equations represent the same line — one must be a constant multiple of the other. Given: $3x + 9y = 10$ and $rx + 18y = 20$. Compare corresponding coefficients.

steps.title: Find the scale factor
steps.content: The $y$-coefficient changes from $9$ to $18$, and the constant changes from $10$ to $20$, so the scale factor is $2$. The $x$-coefficient must also scale by $2$: $$r=2(3)=6.$$

steps.title: Test all four choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — r = 2: Eq 2 becomes $2x + 18y = 20$. But $2 \times (3x+9y=10)$ gives $6x+18y=20$. The x-coefficient 2 ≠ 6. ✗
  • Choice B — r = 3: Eq 2 becomes $3x + 18y = 20$. The x-coefficient 3 ≠ 6. ✗
  • Choice C — r = 6: Eq 2 becomes $6x + 18y = 20 = 2 \times (3x + 9y = 10)$. All three coefficient ratios equal 2. The lines are identical. ✓
  • Choice D — r = 9: Eq 2 becomes $9x + 18y = 20$. The x-coefficient 9 ≠ 6. ✗

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content: Only r = 6 makes the two equations proportional (identical lines), giving infinitely many solutions. That matches choice C — C is correct.
